question.p:
Question 1 of 754: Anatomy
Define anisocytosis ... varying cell sizes

Question 2 of 754: Anatomy
Define poikilocytosis ... varying cell shapes

Question 3 of 754: Anatomy
From which cells do B cells arise? ... stem cells in bone marrow

Question 4 of 754: Anatomy
From which cells do plasma cells differentiate? ... B cells

Question 5 of 754: Anatomy
How can a Nissl stain be used to differentiate microglia from oligodendroglia? ... Microglia are not discernable in a Nissl stain while oligodendroglia appear as small dark nuclei with dark chromatin

Question 6 of 754: Anatomy
In what type of CNS tissue (white or grey) are oligodendroglia predominant? ... white matter

Question 7 of 754: Anatomy
Into what cell type does a monocyte differentiate in tissues? ... Macrophages

Question 8 of 754: Anatomy
Name 2 substances produced by an eosinophil. ... histiminase and arylsulfatase

Question 9 of 754: Anatomy
Name the three types of leukocytic granulocytes. ... basophils, eosinophils, and neutrophils

Question 10 of 754: Anatomy
Name the two types of mononuclear leukocytes. ... lymphocytes and monocytes

Question 11 of 754: Anatomy
What are 2 functions of T cell lymphocytes? ... - cellular immune response - regulation of B lymphocytes and macrophages

Question 12 of 754: Anatomy
What are 2 morphological features of microglia? ... - small irregular nuclei - and relatively little cytoplasm

Question 13 of 754: Anatomy
What are 3 examples of peripheral lymphoid tissue? ... - follicles of lymph nodes - white pulp of spleen - unencapsulated lymphoid tissue

Question 14 of 754: Anatomy
What are 3 functions of a macrophage? ... pagocytosis of bacteria, cell debris, and senescent red cells - scavenges damaged cells and tissues - can function as an antigen presenting cell

Question 15 of 754: Anatomy
What are 3 morphological characteristics of monocytes? ...
- Large
- Kidney-shaped nucleus
- Extensive 'frosted glass' cytoplasm

Question 16 of 754: Anatomy
What are 4 characteristics of the plasma cell morphology? ... - Off center nucleus - Clock face chromatin distribution - Abundant RER - Well developed Golgi apparatus

Question 17 of 754: Anatomy
What are 4 morphologic characteristics of lymphocytes? ... - Round - Small - Densely staining nucleus - Small amount of pale cytoplasm

Question 18 of 754: Anatomy
What are 4 substances contained within the lysosomes of neutrophils? ... - hydrolytic enzymes - lysozyme - myeloperoxidase - lactoferrin

Question 19 of 754: Anatomy
What are 4 types of cells into which T cells differentiate? ... - cytotoxic T cells (MHC I, CD8) - helper T cells (MHCII, CD4) - suppressor T cells - delayed hypersensitivity T cells

Question 20 of 754: Anatomy
What are the 5 important causes for eosinophilia in humans? ... Neoplastic, Asthma, Allergic process, Collagen vascular disease, and Parasites (pneumonic NAACP)

Question 21 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell diffenentiation names of the ACTIVE T CELL line beginning with the pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell? (4) ... - Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell - Lymphoblast - T cell - Active T cell

Question 22 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell differentiation names of the ERYTHROCYTE cell line beginning with pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell? (4) ... - Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell - Proerythroblast - Reticulocyte - Erythrocyte

Question 23 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell differentiation names of the MONOCYTE cell lines beginning with the pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell? (3) ... - Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell - Monoblast - Monocyte

Question 24 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell differentiation names of the NEUTROPHIL, EOSINOPHIL, and BASOPHIL cell lines beginning with the myeloblast stage? (6) ... - Myeloblast - Promyelocyte - Myelocyte - Metamyelocyte - Stab cell - Neutrophil, eosinophil or basophil

Question 25 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell differentiation names of the PLASMA CELL line beginning with the pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell? (4) ... - Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell - Lymphoblast - B cell - Plasma cell

Question 26 of 754: Anatomy
What are the blood cell differentiation names of the PLATELET CELL line beginning with the hematopoietic stem cell? (4) ... - Pluripotento hematopoietic stem cell - Megakaryoblast - Megakaryocyte - Platelets

Question 27 of 754: Anatomy
What are the components of the air-blood barrier? ... - Type I pneumocyte, endothelial cell, and their fused basement membrane.

Question 28 of 754: Anatomy
What are the steps of maturation of a B cell? (2 points) ... - maturation in the marrow - migration to peripheral lymphoid tissue

Question 29 of 754: Anatomy
What are the substances contained within the densly basophilic granules of the basophil? (4) ... - Heparin (anticoagulant) - histamine (vasodilator) - vasoactive amines - Slow reacting substance of anaphylaxis

Question 30 of 754: Anatomy
What are two basic morphological characteristics of neutrophils? ... - multilobed nucleus - large, spherical azurophilic primary granules (lysosomes)

Question 31 of 754: Anatomy
What are two important functions of a neutrophil? ... - acute inflammmatory response of a cell - phagocytosis

Question 32 of 754: Anatomy
What are two names for an increased number of red cells? ... Erythrocytosis and polycythemia

Question 33 of 754: Anatomy
What cell type closely resembles a mast cell? ... basophil

Question 34 of 754: Anatomy
What cranial nerves are commonly involved in an acoustic neuroma? ... CN VII, VIII (association with internal acoustic meatus)

Question 35 of 754: Anatomy
What disease is characterized by destruction of oligodendroglia? ... Multiple sclerosis

Question 36 of 754: Anatomy
What does CD stand for? ... cluster of differentiation

Question 37 of 754: Anatomy
What drug prevents mast cell degranulation? ... Cromolyn sodium

Question 38 of 754: Anatomy
What immunoglobulin can bind to the membrane of a mast cell? ... IgE

Question 39 of 754: Anatomy
What is a reticulocyte? ... a baby (developing) erythrocyte

Question 40 of 754: Anatomy
What is an important example of a Schwannoma? ... Acoustic neuroma

Question 41 of 754: Anatomy
What is another name for pulmonary surfactant? ... DPPC (dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine)

Question 42 of 754: Anatomy
What is percentage of leukocytes in the blood exist as basophils? ... less than 1%

Question 43 of 754: Anatomy
What is the 'gap' between the myelination segment of 2 Schwann cells called? ... Node of Ranvier

Question 44 of 754: Anatomy
What is the advantage of the large surface area:volume ratio in erythrocytes? ... easy gas exchange (Oxygen and Carbon dioxide)

Question 45 of 754: Anatomy
What is the basic morphologic structure of an erythrocyte? ... Anucleate, biconcave

Question 46 of 754: Anatomy
What is the basic morphology of an eosinophil? (2 things) ... - bilobate nucleus - packed with large eosinophilic granules of uniform size

Question 47 of 754: Anatomy
What is the embryologic origin of microglia? ... mesoderm

Question 48 of 754: Anatomy
What is the function of Interferon gamma with relation to macrophages? ... macrophage activation

Question 49 of 754: Anatomy
What is the function of microglia? ... phagocytosis in CNS

Question 50 of 754: Anatomy
What is the function of oligodendroglia? ... myelination of multiple CNS axons

Question 51 of 754: Anatomy
What is the function of pulmonary surfactant? ... lowers alveolar surface tension and prevents atelectasis

Question 52 of 754: Anatomy
What is the function of Schwann cells? ... myelination of PNS (a Schwann cell myelinates only one PNS axon)

Question 53 of 754: Anatomy
What is the importance of the lecithin:sphingomyelin ratio? ... > 2.0 in fetal lung is indicative of fetal lung maturity

Question 54 of 754: Anatomy
What is the importance of the physiologic chloride shift in erythrocytes? ... Membranes contain the chloride bicarbonate antiport allowing the RBC to transport carbon dioxide from the the lung periphery for elimination.

Question 55 of 754: Anatomy
What is the last segment of lung tissue in which ciliated cells are found? ... respiratory bronchioles

Question 56 of 754: Anatomy
What is the last segment of lung tissue in which goblet cells are found? ... terminal broncioles (remember ciliated cells sweep away mucous produced by goblet cells and therefore run deeper)

Question 57 of 754: Anatomy
What is the primary function of a basophil? ... Mediates allergic reactions

Question 58 of 754: Anatomy
What is the primary function of a leukocyte? ... Defense against infections

Question 59 of 754: Anatomy
What is the primary function of a mast cell? ... Mediates allergic reactions

Question 60 of 754: Anatomy
What is the primary function of a plasma cell? ... production of large amounts of a specific antibody to a particular antigen

Question 61 of 754: Anatomy
What is the primary source of energy for erythrocytes? ... glucose (90% anaerobically degraded to lactate, 10% by HMP shunt)

Question 62 of 754: Anatomy
What is the process of degranulation in mast cells? ... release of histamine, heparin, and eosinophil chemotactic factors

Question 63 of 754: Anatomy
What is the range of concentration for leukocytes in the blood? ... 4,000 - 10,000 cells per microliter

Question 64 of 754: Anatomy
What is the response of an eosiniphil to antigen antibody complexes? ... high degree of phagocytosis

Question 65 of 754: Anatomy
What is the response of microglia to tissue dammage? ... transformation into large ameboid phagocytic cells

Question 66 of 754: Anatomy
What is the response to microglia infected with HIV? ... fusion to form multinucleated giant cells in CNS

Question 67 of 754: Anatomy
What is the survival time for an erythrocyte? ... 120 days

Question 68 of 754: Anatomy
What pathognomonic change is seen in neutrophils of a person who is folate/vitamin B12 deficient? ... hypersegmented polys

Question 69 of 754: Anatomy
What percentage of leukocytes exist as eosinophils in the blood? ... 1 - 6%

Question 70 of 754: Anatomy
What percentage of leukocytes exist as neutrophils in the blood? ... 40 - 75%

Question 71 of 754: Anatomy
What percentage of leukocytes in blood are monocytes? ... 2 - 10%

Question 72 of 754: Anatomy
What process occurs when type I pneumocytes are damaged? ... Type II pneumocytes develop into type I

Question 73 of 754: Anatomy
What substance in eosinophilic granules is primarily responsible for defense against helminths and protozoan infections? ... major basic protein

Question 74 of 754: Anatomy
Where is the site of maturation of T lymphocytes? ... Thymus

Question 75 of 754: Anatomy
Which cell type constituitively secretes pulmonary surfactant? ... Type II pneumocyte

Question 76 of 754: Anatomy
Which cell type lines the alveoli? ... Type I pneumocyte

Question 77 of 754: Anatomy
Which leukemia is the result of plasma cell neoplasm? ... Multiple myeloma

Question 78 of 754: Anatomy
Which type of hypersensitivity reaction is a mast cell involved in? ... Type I hypersensitivity reaction

Question 79 of 754: Anatomy
Which type of immunity do B cells exhibit? ... humoral immunity

Question 80 of 754: Anatomy
After arising from the floor of the primitive pharynx, where does the thryoid diverticulum go? ... It descends down into the neck

Question 81 of 754: Anatomy
After the first breath at birth, what causes closure of the ductus arteriosus? ... An increase in oxygen

Question 82 of 754: Anatomy
After the first breath at birth, what causes the closure of the foramen ovale? ... A decrease resistance in pulmonary vasculature causes increased left atrial pressure vs. right atrial pressure

Question 83 of 754: Anatomy
Although the diaphragm descends during development, it maintains innervation from ____? ... C3-C5

Question 84 of 754: Anatomy
An easy pneumonic to remember fetal erythropoiesis is? ... Young Liver Synthesizes Blood

Question 85 of 754: Anatomy
At what time in the course of development is the fetus most susceptible to teratogens? ... Weeks 3-8

Question 86 of 754: Anatomy
Deoxygenated blood from the SVC is expelled into the pulmonary artery and ____ ____ to the lower body of the fetus. ... ductus arteriosus

Question 87 of 754: Anatomy
Do the cardiovascular structures arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 88 of 754: Anatomy
Do the chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 89 of 754: Anatomy
Do the enterochromaffin cells arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 90 of 754: Anatomy
Do the lungs arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 91 of 754: Anatomy
Do the lymphatics arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 92 of 754: Anatomy
Do the melanocytes arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 93 of 754: Anatomy
Do the neural crest cells arise from mesoderm, ectoderm, or endoderm? ... Ectoderm

Question 94 of 754: Anatomy
Do the odontoblasts arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 95 of 754: Anatomy
Do the parafollicular (C) cells of the thyroid arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 96 of 754: Anatomy
Do the Schwann cells arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 97 of 754: Anatomy
Do the urogenital structures arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 98 of 754: Anatomy
Does blood arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 99 of 754: Anatomy
Does bone arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 100 of 754: Anatomy
Does muscle arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 101 of 754: Anatomy
Does the thyroid arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 102 of 754: Anatomy
Does the adrenal cortex arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 103 of 754: Anatomy
Does the ANS arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 104 of 754: Anatomy
Does the celiac ganglion arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 105 of 754: Anatomy
Does the dorsal root ganglion arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 106 of 754: Anatomy
Does the dura connective tissue arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 107 of 754: Anatomy
Does the gut tube epithelium arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 108 of 754: Anatomy
Does the liver arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 109 of 754: Anatomy
Does the pancreas arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 110 of 754: Anatomy
Does the parathyroid arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 111 of 754: Anatomy
Does the pia arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Neural Crest (Ectoderm)

Question 112 of 754: Anatomy
Does the serous linings of body cavities arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 113 of 754: Anatomy
Does the spleen arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Mesoderm

Question 114 of 754: Anatomy
Does the thymus arise from neural crest (ectoderm), mesoderm, or endoderm? ... Endoderm

Question 115 of 754: Anatomy
From what does the ligamentum teres hepatis arise? ... Umbilical vein

Question 116 of 754: Anatomy
How does a bicornate uterus form? ... Results from incomplete fusion of the paramesonephric ducts

Question 117 of 754: Anatomy
How does a cleft lip form? ... Failure of fusion of the maxillary and medial nasal processes

Question 118 of 754: Anatomy
How does a cleft palate form? ... Failure of fusion of the lateral palatine processes, the nasal septum, and/or the median palatine process

Question 119 of 754: Anatomy
How does a horseshoe kidney form? ... Inferior poles of both kidneys fuse, as they ascend from the pelvis during development they get trapped under the inferior mesenteric artery, and remain low in the abdomen

Question 120 of 754: Anatomy
How is meckel's diverticulum different than an omphalomesenteric cyst? ... Omphalomesenteric cyst is a cystic dilatation of the vitelline duct

Question 121 of 754: Anatomy
How long does full development of spermatogenesis take? ... 2 months

Question 122 of 754: Anatomy
How many arteries and veins does the umbilical cord contain? ... - 2 umbilical arteries (carries deoxygenated blood away from fetus) - 1 umbilical vein (oxygenated blood to fetus)

Question 123 of 754: Anatomy
Is a primary spermatocyte 2N or 4N? ... 4N

Question 124 of 754: Anatomy
Is a primary spermatocyte haploid or diploid? ... Diploid, 4N

Question 125 of 754: Anatomy
Is a secondary spermatocyte haploid or diploid? ... Haploid, 2N

Question 126 of 754: Anatomy
Is a secondary spermatocyte N or 2N? ... 2N

Question 127 of 754: Anatomy
Is a speratogonium haploid or diploid? ... Diploid, 2N

Question 128 of 754: Anatomy
Is a spermatid haploid or diploid? ... Haploid, N

Question 129 of 754: Anatomy
Meiosis I is arrested in which phase until ovulation? ... Prophase

Question 130 of 754: Anatomy
Meiosis II is arrested in which phase until fertilization? ... Metaphase (an egg MET a sperm)

Question 131 of 754: Anatomy
Most oxygenated blood reaching the heart via IVC is diverted through the ____ ____ and pumped out the aorta to the head. ... foramen ovale

Question 132 of 754: Anatomy
The right common cardinal vein and right anterior cardinal vein give rise to what adult heart structure? ... Superior vena cava

Question 133 of 754: Anatomy
The stapedius muscle of the ear is formed by which branchial arch? ... 2nd

Question 134 of 754: Anatomy
This type of bone formation consists of ossification of cartilaginous molds and forms long bones at primary and secondary centers. ... Endochondral

Question 135 of 754: Anatomy
True or False, blood in the umbilical vein is 100% saturated with oxygen? ... False, it is 80% saturated

Question 136 of 754: Anatomy
True or False, there are two types of spermatogonia? ... True, type A & type B

Question 137 of 754: Anatomy
What are the 1st branchial arch derivatives innervated by? ... CN V2 and V3

Question 138 of 754: Anatomy
What are the 2nd branchial arch derivatives innervated by? ... CN VII

Question 139 of 754: Anatomy
What are the 3rd branchial arch derivatives innervated by? ... CN IX

Question 140 of 754: Anatomy
What are the 4th and 6th branchial arch derivatives innervated by? ... CN X

Question 141 of 754: Anatomy
What are the cartilage derivatives (5) of the 4th and 6th branchial arches? ... - Thyroid - Cricoid - Arytenoids - Corniculate - Cuneiform

Question 142 of 754: Anatomy
What are the five 2's associated with meckel's diverticulum? ... - 2 inches long - 2 feet from the ileocecal valve - 2% of the population - Commonly presents in the first 2 years of life - May have 2 types of epithelia

Question 143 of 754: Anatomy
What are the rule of 2's for the 2nd week of development? ... - 2 germ layers: epiblast & hypoblast - 2 cavities: amniotic cavity & yolk sac - 2 components to the placenta: cytotrophoblast & syncytiotrophoblast

Question 144 of 754: Anatomy
What are the rule of 3's for the 3rd week of development? ... 3 germ layers (gastrula): ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm

Question 145 of 754: Anatomy
What can a persistent cervical sinus lead to? ... A branchial cyst in the neck

Question 146 of 754: Anatomy
What can be found in the cortex of the thymus? ... It is dense with immature T cells

Question 147 of 754: Anatomy
What can be found in the medulla of the thymus? ... It is pale with mature T cells, epithelial reticular cells, and Hassall's corpuscles

Question 148 of 754: Anatomy
What connects the thyroid diverticulum to the tongue? ... The thyroglossal duct

Question 149 of 754: Anatomy
What devlopmental contributions does the 5th branchial arch make? ... None

Question 150 of 754: Anatomy
What do the 2nd - 4th branchial clefts form, which are obliterated by proliferation of the 2nd arch mesenchyme? ... Temporary cervical sinuses

Question 151 of 754: Anatomy
What does aberrant development of the 3rd and 4th pouches cause? ... DiGeorge's syndrome

Question 152 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 1st aortic arch give rise to? ... Part of the maxillary artery

Question 153 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 2nd pharyngeal pouch develop into? ... Epithelial lining of the palantine tonsils

Question 154 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 3rd aortic arch give rise to? ... Common carotid artery and proximal part of the internal carotid artery

Question 155 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 4th pharyngeal pouch develop into? ... Superior parathyroids

Question 156 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 5th aortic arch give rise to? ... Nothing

Question 157 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 5th pharyngeal pouch develop into? ... C cells of the thyroid

Question 158 of 754: Anatomy
What does the 6th aortic arch give rise to? ... The proximal part of the pulmonary arteries and (on left only) ductus arteriosus

Question 159 of 754: Anatomy
What does the ductus arteriosus give rise to? ... Ligamentum arteriosum

Question 160 of 754: Anatomy
What does the ductus venosus shunt blood away from? ... Liver

Question 161 of 754: Anatomy
What does the first branchial cleft develop into? ... The external auditory meatus

Question 162 of 754: Anatomy
What does the foramen ovale give rise to? ... Fossa ovalis

Question 163 of 754: Anatomy
What does the left 4th aortic arch give rise to? ... Aortic arch

Question 164 of 754: Anatomy
What does the ligamentum venosum come from? ... Ductus venosus

Question 165 of 754: Anatomy
What does the notochord give rise to? ... Nucleus Pulposus

Question 166 of 754: Anatomy
What does the primitive atria give rise to? ... Trabeculated left and right atrium

Question 167 of 754: Anatomy
What does the primitive ventricle give rise to? ... Trabeculated parts of the left and right ventricle

Question 168 of 754: Anatomy
What does the right 4th aortic arch give rise to? ... Proximal part of the right subclavian artery

Question 169 of 754: Anatomy
What does the right horn of the sinus venosus give rise to? ... Smooth part of the right atrium

Question 170 of 754: Anatomy
What does the spleen arise from? ... Dorsal mesentery, but is supplied by the artery of the foregut

Question 171 of 754: Anatomy
What does the thymus arise from? ... Epithelium of the 3rd branchial pouch

Question 172 of 754: Anatomy
What does the thyroid diverticulum arise from? ... The floor of the primitive pharynx

Question 173 of 754: Anatomy
What does the truncus arteriosus give rise to? ... The ascending aorta and pulmonary trunk

Question 174 of 754: Anatomy
What does the umbilical arteries give rise to? ... Medial umbilical ligaments

Question 175 of 754: Anatomy
What ear muscle does the 1st branchial arch form? ... Tensor tympani

Question 176 of 754: Anatomy
What effect does 13-cis-retinoic acid have on the fetus? ... Extremely high risk for birth defects

Question 177 of 754: Anatomy
What effect does ACE inhibitors have on the fetus? ... Renal Damage

Question 178 of 754: Anatomy
What effect does iodide have on the fetus? ... Congenital goiter or hypothyroidism

Question 179 of 754: Anatomy
What effect does warfarin and x-rays have on the fetus? ... Multiple anomalies

Question 180 of 754: Anatomy
What effects does cocaine have on the fetus? ... Abnormal fetal development and fetal addiction

Question 181 of 754: Anatomy
What embryonic structure are the smooth parts of the left and right ventricle derived from? ... Bulbus cordis

Question 182 of 754: Anatomy
What embryonic structure does the coronary sinus come from? ... Left horn of the sinus venosus

Question 183 of 754: Anatomy
What embryonic structure does the median umbilical ligament come from? ... Allantois (urachus)

Question 184 of 754: Anatomy
What fetal landmark has developed within week 2 of fertilization? ... Bilaminar disk

Question 185 of 754: Anatomy
What fetal landmark has occurred within week 1 of fertilization? ... Implantation

Question 186 of 754: Anatomy
What fetal landmark has occurred within week 3 of fertilization? ... Gastrulation

Question 187 of 754: Anatomy
What fetal landmarks (2) have developed within week 3 of fertilization? ... Primitive streak and neural plate begin to form

Question 188 of 754: Anatomy
What five things arise from neuroectoderm? ... - Neurohypophysis - CNS neurons - Oligodendrocytes - Astrocytes - Pineal gland

Question 189 of 754: Anatomy
What four structures make up the diaphragm? ... - Septum transversum - pleuroperitoneal folds - body wall - dorsal mesentery of esophagus

Question 190 of 754: Anatomy
What four things arise from surface ectoderm? ... - Adenohypophysis - Lens of eye - Epithelial linings - Epidermis

Question 191 of 754: Anatomy
What four things does Meckel's cartilage (from the 1st arch) develop into? ... - Mandible - Malleus - Incus - Sphenomandibular ligament

Question 192 of 754: Anatomy
What four things does Reichert's cartilage (from the 2nd arch) develop into? ... - Stapes - Styloid process - Lesser horn of hyoid - Stylohyoid ligament

Question 193 of 754: Anatomy
What four things does the dorsal pancreatic bud become? ... Body, tail, isthmus, and accessory pancreatic duct

Question 194 of 754: Anatomy
What four things does the mesonephric (wolffian) duct develop into? ... - Seminal vesicles - Epididymis - Ejaculatory duct - Ductus deferens

Question 195 of 754: Anatomy
What induces the ectoderm to form the neuroectoderm (neural plate)? ... Notochord

Question 196 of 754: Anatomy
What is a congenital diaphragmatic hernia? ... Abdominal contents herniate into the thorax due to incomplete development of the diaphragm

Question 197 of 754: Anatomy
What is a hypospadias? ... Abnormal opening of penile urethra on inferior side of penis due to failure of urethral folds to close

Question 198 of 754: Anatomy
What is a single umbilical artery associated with? ... Congenital and chromosomal anomalies

Question 199 of 754: Anatomy
What is a urachal cyst or sinus a remnant of? ... The allantois

Question 200 of 754: Anatomy
What is an abnormal opening of penile urethra on superior side of penis due to faulty positioning of the genital tubercle? ... Epispadias

Question 201 of 754: Anatomy
What is associated with an epispadias? ... Exstrophy of the bladder

Question 202 of 754: Anatomy
What is Meckel's diverticulum? ... Persistence of the vitelline duct or yolk sac

Question 203 of 754: Anatomy
What is oligohydramnios associated with? ... Bilateral renal agenesis or posterior urethral valves (in males)

Question 204 of 754: Anatomy
What is oligohydramnios? ... < 0.5 L of amniotic fluid

Question 205 of 754: Anatomy
What is polyhydramnios associated with? ... Esophageal/duodenal atresia, anencephaly

Question 206 of 754: Anatomy
What is polyhydramnios? ... > 1.5-2 L of amniotic fluid

Question 207 of 754: Anatomy
What is Potter's syndrome? ... Bilateral renal agenesis, that results in ologohydramnios causing limb and facial deformities and pulmonary hypoplasia (Babies with Potter's can’t pee in utero)

Question 208 of 754: Anatomy
What is the acrosome of sperm derived from? ... Golgi apparatus

Question 209 of 754: Anatomy
What is the female homologue to the corpus spongiosum in the male? ... Vestibular bulbs

Question 210 of 754: Anatomy
What is the female homologue to the prostate gland in the male? ... Urethral and paraurethral glands (of Skene)

Question 211 of 754: Anatomy
What is the female homologue to the scrotum in the male? ... Labia majora

Question 212 of 754: Anatomy
What is the female homologue to the ventral shaft of the penis in the male? ... Labia minora

Question 213 of 754: Anatomy
What is the flagellum (tail) derived from? ... One of the centrioles

Question 214 of 754: Anatomy
What is the food supply of sperm? ... Fructose

Question 215 of 754: Anatomy
What is the male homologue to the glans clitoris in the female? ... Glans penis

Question 216 of 754: Anatomy
What is the male homologue to the greater vestibular glands (of Bartholin) in the female? ... Bulbourethral glands (of Cowper)

Question 217 of 754: Anatomy
What is the most common congenital anomaly of the GI tract? ... Meckel's diverticulum

Question 218 of 754: Anatomy
What is the most common ectopic thyroid tissue site? ... The tongue

Question 219 of 754: Anatomy
What is the normal remnant of the thyroglossal duct? ... Foramen cecum

Question 220 of 754: Anatomy
What is the postnatal derivative of the notochord? ... The nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disc

Question 221 of 754: Anatomy
What is the site of T-cell maturation? ... Thymus

Question 222 of 754: Anatomy
What part of the gut is the pancreas derived? ... Foregut

Question 223 of 754: Anatomy
What suppresses the development of the paramesonephric ducts in males? ... Mullerian inhibiting substance (secreted by the testes)

Question 224 of 754: Anatomy
What teratogenic agent causes limb defects ('flipper' limbs)? ... Thalidomide

Question 225 of 754: Anatomy
What three structures does the 3rd pharyngeal pouch develop into? ... - Thymus - Left inferior parathyroid - Right inferior parathyroid

Question 226 of 754: Anatomy
What three things does the 1st pharyngeal pouch develop into? ... - Middle ear cavity - Eustachian tube - Mastoid air cells

Question 227 of 754: Anatomy
What three things does the paramesonephric (mullerian) duct develop into? ... - Fallopian tube - Uterus - Part of the vagina

Question 228 of 754: Anatomy
